title: Chapter 17: The Target
content: Aurora Behavioral Health sat on forty acres of Colorado scrubland, fifteen miles outside Boulder, in a building that had been a ski resort before the 2008 financial crisis turned it into a tax write-off and the tax write-off turned it into a psychiatric facility.

The architecture was aggressively rustic — exposed beams, stone fireplaces, floor-to-ceiling windows that looked out over the Flatirons. The marketing materials called it "a sanctuary for healing." The Yelp reviews, written in the same AI-generated cadence as Serenity's, called it "transformative." The patient intake forms, which Layla had pulled from a poorly secured cloud server, called it exactly what it was: a facility where wealthy families from Denver, Boulder, and Aspen sent their daughters when those daughters stopped behaving like assets and started behaving like people.

"Twenty-three current patients," Layla said, reading from her tablet in the Airbnb they'd rented in Boulder — a three-bedroom house with mountain views and, crucially, no cameras. "All women. All under thirty. Same referral pattern as Serenity. Same diagnostic pattern. Oppositional defiant disorder. Adjustment disorder. 'Treatment-resistant' depression that got a lot more treatment-resistant after the patient realized she was being held against her will."
"How many are actually mentally ill?" Sophia asked.
"Based on the intake interviews? Maybe three. The rest are the same as us. Inconvenient. Embarrassing. Refusing to comply."
"And the founder?"

"Two tracks," Rachel said. "Track one: media. Anna Delgado is already working on a follow-up article. She wants patient testimony from Aurora — current patients, former patients, the families who committed them. The more voices we give her, the harder it is for the Family Wellness Alliance to claim this is just 'six extremist women harassing legitimate medical providers.'"
"And track two?"

"Legal. Maria Okonkwo is building a case template — the Sterling prosecution is the model, but the legal framework applies to any facility that committed patients without clinical justification. If we can get one family to flip — one parent who admits they committed their daughter for non-medical reasons — we have a pathway to prosecution."

"Families don't flip," Maya said. She'd been quiet until now, but her voice was sharp. "I've been watching my mother's social media. She's not sorry. She's not even pretending to be sorry. She posted a statement through the Family Wellness Alliance about how she 'only wanted what was best for her daughter' and how 'anti-psychiatry extremists are targeting loving families.' Her Instagram is full of supportive comments from other parents."

"One will flip," Rachel said. "They always do. Not because they're sorry. Because they're scared. Because Sterling's prosecution is going to establish legal precedent. Because once the first parent goes to prison for fraudulent imprisonment, the rest will start looking for plea deals."
"You sound very confident about that."
Rachel's smile was small and sharp. "I was a psychiatrist for fourteen years. I know exactly what happens when people who've spent their lives believing they're untouchable realize they're not."
Layla's fingers stopped tapping. For a long moment, she was perfectly still — the stillness of someone who'd just heard something that unlocked a door they'd thought was permanently sealed.

"I can build that," she said. "I can build a public archive. Secure. Anonymous. Searchable. Every facility, every patient file, every intake interview, every billing record. A database of institutional abuse that anyone can access."
"How long?"
"A month. Maybe less if I can get help."
"We are your help," Sophia said. "Whatever you need."
"I need data. Patient files. Intake records. Billing statements. From every facility. The more we have, the harder it is for them to claim it's all fabricated."

"I can get you the data," Rachel said. "I've been collecting for three years. I know which servers are insecure. I know which facilities use the same cloud provider with the same default passwords. I know which former staff are willing to talk."

"I can protect the operation," Vasquez said. "The Family Wellness Alliance is going to come after us legally. They're going to try to subpoena our communications, our files, our identities. I can make sure they find nothing."

"I can get us press," Chloe said. "Anna Delgado. The Verge. Every outlet that ran the Serenity story. Every journalist who's covered institutional abuse. We give them the archive and they give us the platform."

Maya was quiet for a moment. Then: "And I can get us the legal cover. My aunt is prosecuting Sterling. The precedent she establishes in that case is the foundation for everything else. If Sterling goes to prison for fraudulent imprisonment, every facility that used the same model is legally vulnerable."

Chloe looked around the room — at Layla, her fingers already moving again, already designing the architecture of a system that would expose systems. At Sophia, the porcelain doll turned plaintiff, whose family had responded to her press release by removing her from the will and who had responded to being removed from the will by filing a wrongful commitment suit. At Rachel, who'd spent three years memorizing files in the dark and was now, finally, using them. At Vasquez, the soldier who'd switched sides when she realized which side was committing the crimes. At Maya, the shot-putter who'd learned that trajectories applied to more than just metal balls.

"This is going to take months," Chloe said. "Maybe years. They're going to fight us. The families. The lawyers. The PR firms. Everyone who benefits from the system we're trying to dismantle. They're going to call us crazy and dangerous and anti-psychiatry extremists. They're going to try to discredit us, silence us, lock us up again."
"Let them try," Maya said.
"They already locked us up once," Sophia said. "And we broke out. What makes them think they can do it again?"
Chloe smiled. The weaponized one.
"Nothing," she said. "They have nothing. And we have everything."
That night, Chloe couldn't sleep.

She sat on the balcony of the Boulder Airbnb, looking out at the Flatirons silhouetted against a sky full of stars she couldn't see from Brooklyn. Somewhere out there, in those mountains, was Aurora Behavioral Health — twenty-three women, locked in rooms with fake plants and red camera lights, being told that their refusal to comply was a symptom of their illness.
She pulled out her phone. Opened TikTok. Searched for #AuroraBehavioralHealth.

One result. A video posted three weeks ago, with twelve views and zero comments. A girl — maybe nineteen, maybe twenty — sitting in what looked like a patient room, the same dove-gray loungewear Chloe had worn for fourteen days. She wasn't speaking. She was just looking at the camera, her expression somewhere between pleading and defeated. The caption was three words:
*please someone help*
Chloe watched it five times. Then she opened her drafts and started typing.

She didn't know if the girl would see it. She didn't know if anyone in Aurora had internet access. She didn't know if the video was even still up — facilities like Serenity confiscated phones on intake.
But she posted it anyway.
*We see you. We're coming. Hold on.*
Then she tagged it: #WomenOfSerenity #AuroraBehavioralHealth #WeSeeYou
She didn't expect it to go viral. She didn't expect the girl to see it.

But four hundred miles away, in a facility whose founder had just died, whose new management was uncertain, whose security protocols were three weeks out of date — a girl named Jamie opened TikTok on a phone an orderly had forgotten to confiscate, and saw three words that made her start to cry.
*We see you.*
